section.title: Macedonia, Troas, and the Farewell at Miletus
section.sectionRationale: The third missionary journey draws to a close. Paul revisits the churches of Macedonia and Greece, raises Eutychus at Troas on the Lord's Day, and, sailing past Ephesus to make Jerusalem by Pentecost, summons the Ephesian elders to Miletus for a farewell that is really a charge: guard the flock God bought with His own blood.
section.thesis: As Paul sets his face for Jerusalem and chains, he hands the churches over to God and to the word of His grace, entrusting the flock to elders whom the Spirit has made overseers to shepherd the church that Christ purchased with His own blood.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the road through Macedonia and Greece; Eutychus raised at Troas; the coasting voyage to Miletus; Paul's example and his Spirit-driven course to Jerusalem; the charge to guard the flock; and the tearful parting on the shore.

units.label: Through Macedonia and Greece
units.note: revisiting the churches, evading a plot

verses.text: When the uproar had ended, Paul sent for the disciples. And after encouraging them, he said goodbye to them and left for Macedonia.
After traveling through that area and speaking many words of encouragement, he arrived in Greece,
where he stayed three months. And when the Jews formed a plot against him as he was about to sail for Syria, he decided to go back through Macedonia.
Paul was accompanied by Sopater son of Pyrrhus from Berea, Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica, Gaius from Derbe, Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus from the province of Asia.
These men went on ahead and waited for us in Troas.
And after the Feast of Unleavened Bread, we sailed from Philippi, and five days later we rejoined them in Troas, where we stayed seven days.

units.label: Eutychus raised at Troas
units.note: the first day of the week, life restored

verses.text: On the first day of the week we came together to break bread. Since Paul was ready to leave the next day, he talked to them and kept on speaking until midnight.
Now there were many lamps in the upper room where we were gathered.
And a certain young man named Eutychus, seated in the window, was sinking into a deep sleep as Paul talked on and on. When he was sound asleep, he fell from the third story and was picked up dead.
But Paul went down, threw himself on the young man, and embraced him. “Do not be alarmed!” he said. “He is still alive!”
Then Paul went back upstairs, broke bread, and ate. And after speaking until daybreak, he departed.
And the people were greatly relieved to take the boy home alive.

units.label: The coasting voyage to Miletus
units.note: hurrying to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost

verses.text: We went on ahead to the ship and sailed to Assos, where we were to take Paul aboard. He had arranged this because he was going there on foot.
And when he met us at Assos, we took him aboard and went on to Mitylene.
Sailing on from there, we arrived the next day opposite Chios. The day after that we arrived at Samos, and on the following day we came to Miletus.
Paul had decided to sail past Ephesus to avoid spending time in the province of Asia, because he was in a hurry to reach Jerusalem, if possible, by the day of Pentecost.
From Miletus, Paul sent to Ephesus for the {{presbyteros|elders}} of the church.

units.label: Paul's example and his course to Jerusalem
units.note: the whole will of God, compelled by the Spirit

verses.text: When they came to him, he said, “You know how I lived the whole time I was with you, from the first day I arrived in the province of Asia.
I served the Lord with great humility and with tears, especially in the trials that came upon me through the plots of the Jews.
I did not shrink back from declaring anything that was helpful to you as I taught you publicly and from house to house,
testifying to Jews and Greeks alike about repentance to God and faith in our Lord Jesus Christ.
And now, compelled by the Spirit, I am going to Jerusalem, not knowing what will happen to me there.
I only know that in town after town the Holy Spirit warns me that chains and afflictions await me.
But I consider my life of no value to me, if only I may finish my course and complete the ministry I have received from the Lord Jesus, the ministry of testifying to the good news of God’s grace.
Now I know that none of you among whom I have preached the kingdom will see my face again.
Therefore I testify to you this day that I am innocent of the blood of all men.
For I did not shrink back from declaring to you the whole will of God.

units.label: Guard the flock God bought
units.note: the church purchased with His own blood

verses.text: Keep watch over yourselves and the entire flock of which the Holy Spirit has made you {{episkopos|overseers}}. {{poimaino|Be shepherds}} of the church of God, which He purchased with His own blood.
I know that after my departure, savage wolves will come in among you and will not spare the flock.
Even from your own number, men will rise up and distort the truth to draw away disciples after them.
Therefore be alert and remember that for three years I never stopped warning each of you night and day with tears.
And now I commit you to God and to the word of His {{charis|grace}}, which can build you up and give you an inheritance among all who are sanctified.
I have not coveted anyone’s silver or gold or clothing.
You yourselves know that these hands of mine have ministered to my own needs and those of my companions.
In everything, I showed you that by this kind of hard work we must help the weak, remembering the words of the Lord Jesus Himself: ‘It is more blessed to give than to receive.’”

units.label: The tearful farewell
units.note: kneeling to pray on the shore

verses.text: When Paul had said this, he knelt down with all of them and prayed.
They all wept openly as they embraced Paul and kissed him.
They were especially grieved by his statement that they would never see his face again. Then they accompanied him to the ship.

presbyteros.gloss: Elder; a mature, appointed leader in the church. Paul sends to Ephesus for the presbyteroi, and in the same speech calls these very men episkopoi, overseers, and charges them to shepherd, so the three terms describe one office seen from different angles.

episkopos.gloss: Overseer, guardian, one who watches over. The Holy Spirit, not merely the church, has made the elders overseers; their authority is a stewardship received from God to guard His people against wolves and distortion.

poimaino.gloss: To shepherd, tend, and feed a flock. The word gives us pastor. The elders are to do for the church what a shepherd does for sheep: feed, protect, and lead, under Christ the Chief Shepherd who owns the flock.

charis.gloss: Grace, undeserved favor. Paul commits the elders not to himself but to God and to the word of His grace, the gospel message that has power to build them up and secure their inheritance among the sanctified.

basic.context: Paul is finishing his third journey. He revisits the churches of Macedonia and Greece, and on the way back stops at Troas, where a young man named Eutychus falls to his death from a window during a long night sermon and Paul restores him alive. Hurrying to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost, Paul sails past Ephesus but calls its elders to meet him at Miletus for a farewell speech, the one full address to Christians in Acts. He is going to chains, and he wants the shepherds ready.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative, but the Miletus speech is teaching within the narrative, the closest thing in Acts to a pastoral letter spoken aloud. Read it as Paul's charge to church leaders in every age: watch yourselves and the flock, expect opposition from without and from within, and rest the church on God and the word of His grace rather than on any human leader.
basic.originalLanguages: One office is named three ways. Paul sends for the presbyteroi, elders, then says the Spirit made them episkopoi, overseers, and tells them to poimainein, to shepherd. Elder, overseer, and pastor are the same men and the same office. He commits them to the word of God's charis, His grace, which alone can build them up.
basic.typology: Paul the departing shepherd who warns of wolves and commends the flock to God stands in a long line: Moses, Joshua, and Samuel all gave farewell charges to God's people. Above them all stands Christ the Good Shepherd, who unlike Paul does not merely warn of danger but lays down His life for the sheep and buys them with His own blood.
basic.christ: The center of the speech is verse 28: the church of God, which He purchased with His own blood. The flock is precious because of the price paid for it, the blood of Christ. Every faithful pastor since serves a people bought at that cost. Paul's own readiness to face chains and death for the ministry mirrors the self-giving of the Lord Jesus he preaches.
basic.god: God the Holy Spirit compels Paul toward Jerusalem, warns him of chains, and appoints the elders as overseers. God the Father owns the church and purchases it. Paul entrusts everything to God and to the word of His grace, confident that God, not Paul, keeps the church.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 5:1-4 gives the same charge, elders shepherding the flock under the Chief Shepherd. Ephesians 4:11 lists pastors and teachers as Christ's gift. John 10 shows the Good Shepherd laying down His life. Ezekiel 34 warns of false shepherds who fail the flock God will Himself come to save.
basic.oneStory: As the gospel presses on from Jerusalem toward Rome, Paul makes sure the churches he planted will stand after he is gone. He hands them to God and to the word of grace and to shepherds who must guard the blood-bought flock. The mission outlives the missionary because the church belongs to Christ, who purchased it and keeps it.

context.literary: The chapter closes the third missionary journey and opens the long road to Jerusalem and Rome. It contains one of the we passages, marking Luke's presence, and the Miletus address, the only extended speech to Christians in Acts. Its structure mirrors Paul's letters: reminder of his conduct, warning of danger, and commendation to God.
context.historical: Around AD 57, Paul is gathering the collection for Jerusalem and traveling with delegates from the churches. Ephesus was the great city of the province of Asia, where Paul had labored three years. Elders led each local church; Miletus lay about thirty miles south of Ephesus, a natural port for a hurried meeting.
context.canonical: The speech is the fullest New Testament picture of the pastoral office and stands beside 1 Peter 5 and the Pastoral Epistles. Its identification of elder, overseer, and shepherd as one office, and its ground of the church in the purchasing blood of God's own Son, have shaped Christian understanding of ministry ever since.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke records Paul's farewell to show how the apostle handed his churches on: by personal example, by warning of coming dangers, and by committing the flock to God and to qualified, Spirit-appointed shepherds who guard the people Christ bought.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The travel details describe Paul's particular journey. The charge to the elders is prescriptive for church leaders in every age: watch yourselves first, then the flock; feed and guard it; expect opposition; and rest it on God and the word of His grace, not on yourselves.

debates.question: Are elder, overseer, and pastor three offices or one?

views.label: One office, three terms
views.case: In this one speech Paul calls the same men elders, says the Spirit made them overseers, and commands them to shepherd. Titus 1:5-7 likewise moves from elder to overseer for the same person, and 1 Peter 5:1-2 has elders exercising oversight and shepherding. The strongest case is that the words describe one office by its dignity, its duty of oversight, and its shepherd care.

views.label: Distinct offices or ranks
views.case: Later church history distinguished bishop, presbyter, and pastor into separate orders, and some read a developing hierarchy already implied. The strongest case points to the practical need for order and to the singular the overseer in the Pastorals as a recognized position.

debates.bearing: If the terms name one office, then every faithful pastor is an elder charged to oversee and shepherd, accountable to guard the flock; the passage levels ministry under Christ the Chief Shepherd rather than building a ladder of ranks.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary of ministry is gathered in a single scene. Paul summons the presbyteroi, the elders, then tells them the Holy Spirit has set them as episkopoi, overseers, and charges them to poimainein, to shepherd, the flock. Elder names their standing, overseer their task of watchful guarding, shepherd their tender feeding and leading. He does not commit them to himself but to God and to the word of His charis, grace, the gospel that builds up and secures an inheritance. Above them the church is God's own, purchased with His own blood.

typology.fulfillment: Like Moses in Deuteronomy and Joshua and Samuel, Paul gives a final charge before he goes; all such charges point to Christ, whose farewell to His own in John 14 to 17 secures them by the Spirit He sends.
The elders shepherd the flock as under-shepherds of Christ the Good Shepherd, John 10, and the Chief Shepherd, 1 Peter 5:4, who lays down His life for the sheep.
A young man dead is restored alive, a sign in miniature of the resurrection life the gospel brings, foreshadowing the raising of all who are Christ's.

study.christ: Everything in the charge rests on verse 28: the church of God, which He purchased with His own blood. Christ is the buyer, and His blood is the price; the flock is precious beyond measure because of what it cost Him. The elders shepherd under Him, the Chief Shepherd. Paul's willingness to spend his life and face chains for the ministry of God's grace is itself a witness to the self-giving Lord he serves, the one who first gave Himself for the sheep.
study.god: The whole passage is God-centered. The Holy Spirit compels Paul, warns him city by city, and appoints the overseers. God the Father owns and purchases the church. Paul entrusts the elders to God and to the word of His grace, confident that the keeping of the church is God's work, not his. The mission goes forward because God, not the apostle, holds the flock.

crossReferences.note: Elders are to shepherd the flock of God, exercising oversight, under the Chief Shepherd, the same fusion of elder, overseer, and shepherd.
The Good Shepherd lays down His life for the sheep; the ground of the elders' care is Christ's own self-giving.
God rebukes shepherds who fail the flock and promises to shepherd them Himself, fulfilled in Christ and continued through faithful elders.
The qualifications for the overseer, showing the single office Paul here entrusts with the flock.

study.oneStory: The gospel is moving from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, and Paul is nearing the end of his part in it. So he secures the churches for what comes after him: he commits them to God and to the word of His grace and charges Spirit-appointed shepherds to guard the flock that Christ bought with His own blood. The mission outlasts the missionary because the church belongs to the Lord who purchased it, and He will keep it to the end.

quiz.q: Why does Paul call the church so precious in verse 28?

options.text: Because God purchased it with His own blood.
options.note: Paul grounds the flock's worth in the price Christ paid, His own blood.

options.text: Because the Holy Spirit appointed overseers to lead it.
options.note: True and stated in the verse, but the reason for its preciousness is the purchasing blood, not the appointment of leaders.

options.text: Because Paul had labored among them for three years.
options.note: Paul's long labor is real, but the flock's value rests on Christ's purchase, not on Paul's effort.

options.text: Because the church in Ephesus was large and influential.
options.note: Its worth is not size or influence but the blood that bought it.

quiz.q: What do the words elder, overseer, and shepherd describe in this speech?

options.text: One office named three ways: standing, oversight, and shepherd care.
options.note: Paul calls the same men elders, says the Spirit made them overseers, and tells them to shepherd.

options.text: The same leaders, with shepherding as their main daily task.
options.note: Rightly sees them as the same men, but narrows the point; the terms together name one office in its full scope.

options.text: Three separate ranks of leadership in the church.
options.note: Later tradition distinguished ranks, but here the terms fall on the same men, not on a hierarchy.

options.text: Three unrelated groups who happened to gather at Miletus.
options.note: The text addresses one group, the Ephesian elders, throughout.

quiz.q: How does Paul say the elders should guard the flock?

options.text: By watching themselves and the flock, and resting the church on God and the word of His grace.
options.note: Paul charges self-watch first, then vigilance for the flock, and commits them to God and His grace.

options.text: By staying alert against wolves who come from outside the church.
options.note: True, but incomplete; Paul warns that some will rise from within their own number too.

options.text: By collecting silver and gold to fund the ministry.
options.note: Paul says the opposite, that he coveted no one's silver and worked with his hands.

options.text: By relying on Paul's ongoing presence to keep them safe.
options.note: Paul is leaving and commits them not to himself but to God and the word of His grace.

quiz.q: What compels Paul toward Jerusalem despite the warnings?

options.text: The Spirit compels him, and he counts his life of no value if he may finish his ministry.
options.note: Paul says he is bound in the Spirit and holds his life cheap to complete his course.

options.text: He wants to deliver the collection and see the church there.
options.note: True to his wider plans, but here he names the Spirit's compulsion and his resolve to finish the ministry.

options.text: He is confident nothing bad will happen to him there.
options.note: The reverse: the Spirit warns him city by city that chains and afflictions await.

options.text: He is fleeing the plot of the Jews in Greece.
options.note: The plot changed his route earlier, but it is not what drives him to Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What happened to Eutychus at Troas?

options.text: He fell from a window and died, and Paul restored him alive.
options.note: The young man was picked up dead, and Paul embraced him and declared him alive.

options.text: He fell asleep during the sermon and was gently woken.
options.note: He did sink into sleep, but the point is that he fell to his death and was raised.

options.text: He was injured in the fall but recovered on his own over time.
options.note: The text says he was taken up dead and then restored, not that he slowly recovered.

options.text: He was unharmed and simply went home early.
options.note: He fell from the third story and was picked up dead before being restored.

questions: Paul told the elders to keep watch over themselves first, and then the flock. Why does guarding your own soul have to come before caring for others, and how are you doing at it?
The church is precious because it was bought with Christ's own blood. How would seeing other believers as blood-bought change the way you treat them this week?
Paul warned that danger would come both from savage wolves outside and from men within their own number. Where do you see the church today needing that same alertness?
Paul committed the elders not to himself but to God and to the word of His grace. What are you tempted to lean on for security instead of God and His word?
Paul said it is more blessed to give than to receive and worked with his own hands to help the weak. Where is God calling you to give rather than to grasp?
The Ephesians wept most because they would not see Paul's face again, yet the mission went on. How does it comfort you that the church belongs to Christ and not to any one leader?

translationNotes.note: The BSB prints an em dash before the ministry of testifying to the good news of God's grace; it is rendered here with a comma for a clean reading, with no change of meaning.
The church of God which He purchased with His own blood carries a footnoted alternative, with the blood of His own Son. Either way the flock is bought at the price of Christ's blood.
One Greek office is named by three words in this verse and its context: elder, overseer, and shepherd, describing the same men in their standing, their oversight, and their pastoral care.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 20. Footnote markers and line breaks were skipped when building verse text. One em dash in verse 24 was converted to a comma; noted in translationNotes. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
